identity.title: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events without Facilities

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly organize, promote, book, produce, or manage live performing-arts, sports, concert, festival, fair, and similar events in third-party facilities for artists, rights holders, sponsors, venue partners, and event audiences.
lens.excluded_activities: Single-event shells, passive financing vehicles, captive internal promotion units, operators of the facilities themselves, performing-arts producers and sports teams classified elsewhere, and firms whose activity is non-live media or convention and trade-show organization.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Promoters contract with artists, booking agents, rights holders, venue operators, sponsors, and production vendors. Revenue is commonly ticket-led, with possible service charges, sponsorship, concessions or merchandise participation, camping and other event fees; costs include artist guarantees or profit shares, venue rental, marketing, and production services.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes both repeat outsourced promotion/management businesses and event-specific entities taking proprietary ticket risk. The lens retains repeat operators with external artist, rights-holder, sponsor, venue, or audience relationships and excludes single-event shells because they are not durable recurring service firms.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation and wage mix only for combined NAICS 711300, which includes facility operators, while Census reports AI task use across all U.S. employer businesses. I mapped exposure to the combined industry's planning, marketing, sales, public relations, writing, customer-service, finance, scheduling, and administrative work, using Census evidence that writing, document analysis, and information search lead current generative-AI task use. Removing facility-heavy security, ushering, maintenance, food-service, and grounds work likely makes the combined BLS mix understate exposure for 711320.
a.rationale: The combined industry has wage-intensive management, business, finance, marketing, creative, sales, and office roles. BLS shows management at 10.79% of employment, business and financial operations at 9.77%, arts/media at 10.97%, sales at 5.35%, and office support at 8.95%; detailed roles include event planners, market researchers, producers, public-relations specialists, customer-service representatives, accountants, and administrative staff. Without facilities, more of the promoter's payroll is likely concentrated in these information workflows, although artist negotiation, relationship judgment, live production, and field coordination remain human-heavy.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-task study from independent 711320 promoters, separating already automated work from current manual work across booking, marketing, ticketing, settlement, sponsorship, finance, and production coordination, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The available occupation mix combines promoters with and without facilities.
Economy-wide AI task use identifies deployed use cases, not the full technically exposed share.
Freelancers and outsourced production labor may not appear consistently in employer payroll data.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures AI adoption and task use across all nonfarm employer businesses, not implemented substitution within independent event promoters. I use its 18% firm adoption, 32% employment-weighted adoption, concentration in sales/marketing and strategy, limited functional breadth, and rare reported employment reductions as the starting point, then allow five years for diffusion in digitally mediated promoter workflows. The all-economy sample may understate marketing-oriented adoption but may overstate the ability of small promoters to integrate fragmented ticketing, CRM, vendor, and finance systems.
rho.rationale: Promotion has implementable digital workflows in campaign production, fan segmentation, sponsor proposals, customer responses, contract extraction, research, schedule coordination, reconciliation, and reporting. However, Census finds 57% of adopting firms use AI in no more than three functions, 66% rely solely on augmentation, and only 2% report AI-related employment decreases. Artist and venue negotiations, brand risk, rights clearance, live-event exceptions, and approval controls keep realized labor avoidance below exposure.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operating data from promoter CRM, ticketing, marketing, contract, and event-settlement systems, tied to paid hours, vendor spend, campaign throughput, errors, and staffing by function, would materially move implementation expectations.
caveats: BTOS is not industry-specific and its AI question was broadened in late 2025.
Use of AI is not equivalent to reliable end-to-end workflow implementation.
Seasonal and project-based staffing may turn productivity into greater event capacity rather than lower headcount.

e.rationale: The official definition is centered on organizing, promoting, and managing live events in facilities operated by others and includes theatrical booking agencies, which aligns closely with repeat external service. Eligibility is nevertheless reduced for single-event entities, firms built around nonrepeat proprietary festivals, owner-personal relationships with no transferable organization, and businesses whose normalized earnings do not represent an operating service platform. At LMM scale, a majority of firms are likely to run multiple events or tours and maintain repeat artist, venue, sponsor, or rights-holder relationships.
e.change_evidence: A verified sample of LMM 711320 firms classified by event count, repeat-client revenue, proprietary versus third-party event economics, owner dependence, legal-entity structure, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source measures eligibility under the exact recurring-service and EBITDA lens.
Ticket-risk promotion can look like a service relationship while economically resembling principal investing in events.
Classification and financial statements may be distorted by event-specific subsidiaries and gross ticket accounting.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans across U.S. business owners, not completed control transfers among eligible LMM promoters. Its 22% figure for employer-business owners is discounted for plan noncompletion, relationship dependence, event-specific entities, contingent liabilities, and transfers that are closures or internal reorganizations. The broad survey includes much smaller firms, which may understate LMM marketability, but intentions may overstate realized qualifying deals.
s5.rationale: Promoters can be transferred when teams, brands, contracts, calendars, sponsor relationships, data, and production processes survive the founder. Broad employer-owner intentions support meaningful availability, and the largest industry participant reports recurring acquisitions, but artist relationships, guarantees, change-of-control provisions, and key-person dependence can impair transferability.
s5.change_evidence: A completed-deal census from PitchBook, Axial, business brokers, entertainment lawyers, and promoter associations, matched to 711320 firms and tagged for control, owner exit, event portfolio, and EBITDA, would materially change the probability.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions rather than completed deals and is cross-industry.
Live Nation is a global strategic buyer and is not representative of LMM transaction frequency.
Acquisitions of event brands or assets without operating control should be excluded.

q.rationale: Promoters typically earn ticket revenue while paying artists fixed guarantees, shares of ticket sales, or event-profit percentages and bearing venue and production costs. Live Nation states that artists receive the majority of ticketing revenue, event costs are substantial relative to promotion revenue, and promotion revenue changes do not ordinarily translate proportionally to operating income. Internal workflow savings can be retained during an event cycle, but competition for artists and events, renegotiated guarantees and profit shares, sponsor pricing, and consumer ticket-price pressure should transfer a sizable portion over five years.
q.change_evidence: Event-level contribution-margin data linked to artist agreements, venue contracts, pricing, renewals, promoter bids, and labor savings before and after automation across independent promoters would materially change retained benefit.
caveats: No source directly observes pass-through of AI-enabled labor savings.
Retention differs between fixed guarantees, profit shares, fee-for-service promotion, and proprietary event brands.
The public-company evidence combines promotion with venue, ticketing, sponsorship, and international operations.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for combined promoters and agents (NAICS 7113-4) from $83.4 billion in 2024 to $103.6 billion in 2034, a 2.2% compound annual rate; applying that rate for five years yields about 1.11. The aggregate includes promoters with facilities, promoters without facilities, and agents, and output is not a pure constant-quality quantity measure. A large global promoter's 10% year-over-year increase in advance ticket sales for 2026 supports current demand direction but likely overstates independent U.S. LMM growth.
d5.rationale: The base case follows the official real-output trajectory. Live Nation's 2025 filing reports strong advance ticket demand, but independent promoters remain exposed to local event supply, discretionary spending, artist costs, venue availability, and competition. The range allows a cyclical contraction or event failures as well as continued growth in live attendance and event frequency.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price, same-promoter panel from Pollstar, ticketing platforms, and independent promoter financials covering event count, tickets sold, attendance, cancellations, and promoter service revenue would materially change the five-year demand ratio.
caveats: BLS output is broader than 711320 and the projection horizon is ten years.
Live Nation is global, vertically integrated, and much larger than the screened firms.
Gross ticket revenue can move differently from constant-quality promoter service quantity.

o.rationale: A promoter remains accountable for artist or rights-holder contracting, demand forecasting, marketing plans, ticket economics, venue rental, production procurement, permits and insurance coordination, scheduling, sponsor delivery, and live-event exception management. Software and AI can enable artist self-promotion, automate campaign execution, and let venues or rights holders internalize simple events, so a meaningful share can be self-served. Complex tours, festivals, and scaled public events still require an organization to carry financial, contractual, and execution responsibility.
o.change_evidence: A study of events shifted from third-party promoters to artist, venue, or rights-holder self-service, segmented by event size and complexity and tracking failures, economics, insurance, and accountability, would materially change the operator-required share.
caveats: The share of events that can be self-promoted is not directly measured.
Artist and venue sophistication varies widely, as does access to ticketing and production platforms.
Accountability may migrate to venues, managers, or software-enabled agencies rather than disappear.

narrative.executive_view: The eligible core is a repeat promoter or booking organization that coordinates live events in third-party venues and can survive beyond a single event or founder. Its work is more information-intensive than facility operation, creating meaningful AI exposure, but ticket-risk economics, artist bargaining power, and relationship dependence constrain implementation, retention, and transferability.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate artist and market research, campaign and creative variants, sponsor proposals, fan segmentation, customer-service triage, schedule coordination, contract extraction, production briefs, reconciliation, and post-event reporting. Human judgment remains central in talent relationships, event selection, negotiations, brand control, live production, crisis response, and final financial approvals.
narrative.value_capture: Savings in promoter overhead can improve event contribution margin without an immediate ticket-price change. Over time, however, competition for artists and rights, profit-sharing structures, stronger guarantees, venue negotiations, sponsor pricing, and consumer scrutiny can pass part of the benefit away. The ticket-led model is not a simple fixed-fee service model.
narrative.firm_availability: Most scaled firms likely operate repeat calendars or tours and therefore fit the recurring-service lens, but single-event shells and founder-centric portfolios must be excluded. Broad employer-owner surveys indicate meaningful five-year transfer intent, while promoter contracts, guarantees, key relationships, and event liabilities make completed control transfers less certain.
narrative.demand_durability: Official projections imply modest real growth for the broader promoter-and-agent market, and current large-company advance sales support continued appetite for live events. Demand is still discretionary, seasonal, and volatile; local venue capacity, artist supply, cancellations, economic slowdowns, and rising guarantees can reduce promoter quantity even when headline ticket revenue grows.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Six-digit evidence is weak for occupation mix, recurring-service eligibility, independent-promoter contracts, and completed transactions. A bad target would be a collection of event-specific entities with founder-owned relationships, concentrated artist or festival exposure, large guarantees, weak data rights, and no repeat operational team. AI may raise campaign volume or service quality rather than reduce labor, and self-service may erode simpler promotion assignments.
narrative.principal_driver: Booking, marketing, ticketing, sponsorship, contracting, coordination, and settlement create a concentrated set of AI-addressable information workflows around durable demand for live events.
narrative.principal_weakness: Promoter economics are volatile and relationship-driven, with artists and venues capturing much of the value and single-event structures weakening eligibility and transferability.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 711300
sources.supports: Combined promoter-industry occupation and wage mix: management 10.79% of employment, business/financial operations 9.77%, arts/media 10.97%, sales 5.35%, and office support 8.95%; detailed roles include 5,160 event planners, 2,930 market-research and marketing specialists, 3,300 customer-service representatives, and 1,890 accountants and auditors.

sources.title: NAICS 711320 Definition: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events without Facilities
sources.supports: Official industry scope: organizing, promoting, or managing live performing-arts productions, sports events, fairs, concerts, festivals, and similar events in facilities operated by others; theatrical booking agencies are included.

sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks
sources.supports: National BTOS evidence: 18% of firms used AI in a business function and 32% employment-weighted; sales and marketing was the most common function among adopters at 52%; writing, document analysis, and information search led task use; 66% used AI solely for augmentation and only 2% reported employment decreases.

sources.title: Live Nation Entertainment 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Observed promoter model: promoters contract with artists or booking agents, earn primarily ticket revenue, pay guarantees or revenue/profit shares, rent third-party venues, arrange production, and bear event risk; artists receive the majority of ticketing revenue and promotion costs are substantial. The filing also reports recurring acquisitions and 2026 ticket sales 10% above the prior-year point.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 U.S. owner survey: 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; 17% of owners age 55 or older and 10% of younger owners reported sale or transfer plans.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects real output for promoters of performing arts and sports and agents for public figures from $83.4 billion in 2024 to $103.6 billion in 2034, a 2.2% compound annual increase; employment in 711300 is projected to rise 6.8%.
